 {"id":"automated_plan_reviser_pro-0fm","title":"Unit Tests: Update and Download Functions (check_for_updates, cmd_update, download validation)","description":"# Task: Unit Tests for Update Functions

## Objective
Test self-update functionality with REAL network behavior (controlled endpoints).

## Functions to Test

### 1. check_for_updates() - Daily Update Check
```bash
# Test cases:
- APR_CHECK_UPDATES not set → skip check
- APR_CHECK_UPDATES=1 → perform check
- Already checked today (timestamp file) → skip
- Older than 24h → check again
- Remote version newer → show update message
- Remote version same → no message
- Remote version older → no message
- Network timeout → graceful failure, no crash

# Timestamp file:
- Created in $APR_HOME/.last_update_check
- Contains Unix timestamp
- Updated after each check
```

### 2. cmd_update() - Self-Update Command
```bash
# Test with mock HTTP server or controlled URLs:
# Note: We can use a local HTTP server for "real" testing

# Version checking:
- Fetch VERSION file
- Parse remote version correctly
- Compare with current VERSION
- Already up to date message
- Newer version available message

# Download and verification:
- Download apr script
- Verify bash shebang (^#!.*bash regex)
- Checksum verification when available
- Checksum format: "hash" or "hash  filename"
- Handle missing checksum gracefully

# Installation:
- Detect installation path from BASH_SOURCE
- Handle writable path (mv)
- Handle non-writable path (sudo mv)
- Permission preservation

# Error cases:
- Network error → EXIT_NETWORK_ERROR
- Download failure → EXIT_UPDATE_ERROR
- Invalid shebang → EXIT_UPDATE_ERROR
- Checksum mismatch → EXIT_UPDATE_ERROR
- Permission denied → error message

# Cleanup:
- Temp directory created via APR_TEMP_DIR
- Temp directory cleaned on success
- Temp directory cleaned on failure
```

### 3. Shebang Validation
```bash
# Test cases:
- "#!/bin/bash" → valid
- "#!/usr/bin/env bash" → valid
- "#!/usr/bin/bash" → valid
- "#!/bin/sh" → invalid
- "#!/usr/bin/env sh" → invalid
- Empty file → invalid
- Binary file → invalid
- Script with BOM → handle gracefully
```

## Testing Approach
```bash
# Use local HTTP server for controlled testing:
# Start server with test files
python3 -m http.server 8888 --directory tests/fixtures/update/ &
export VERSION_URL="http://localhost:8888/VERSION"
export RELEASES_URL="http://localhost:8888/releases"

# Test fixtures:
tests/fixtures/update/
├── VERSION           # Contains "2.0.0"
├── releases/
│   └── download/
│       └── v2.0.0/
│           ├── apr       # Valid bash script
│           └── apr.sha256
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] check_for_updates respects opt-in flag
- [ ] Daily check throttling works
- [ ] cmd_update downloads and validates correctly
- [ ] Shebang validation catches invalid scripts
- [ ] Checksum verification works (both formats)
- [ ] Error handling covers all failure modes
- [ ] Cleanup happens on all exit paths","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-12T20:08:59.666220376-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-12T20:08:59.666220376-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"automated_plan_reviser_pro-0fm","depends_on_id":"automated_plan_reviser_pro-hqt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-12T20:12:37.337877255-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

## Objective
Test the run command comprehensively using dry-run and render modes (no actual Oracle calls).

## Test Scenarios

### 1. Dry Run Mode (--dry-run)
apr run 1 --dry-run
Verify output shows:
- Oracle command that would be executed
- Model selection
- File arguments
- Slug format
- Output file path
- All flags passed correctly

### 2. Render Mode (--render)
apr run 1 --render
Verify:
- Prompt content rendered to stdout
- Files would be included
- No Oracle process started

### 3. Render with Copy (--render --copy)
apr run 1 --render --copy
Verify clipboard integration (if available)

### 4. Preflight Validation
Test successful preflight: apr run 1 --dry-run
- Verify: All pre-flight checks passed
Test failed preflight (missing file):
- Verify: Error message, non-zero exit
Test skip preflight: apr run 1 --dry-run --no-preflight
- Verify: Skips checks, continues

### 5. Include Implementation Flag
apr run 1 --dry-run --include-impl
Verify:
- Implementation file in command args
- Slug includes -with-impl
- Correct template loaded

### 6. Workflow Selection
apr run 1 --dry-run -w myworkflow
apr run 1 --dry-run --workflow myworkflow
Verify correct workflow loaded

### 7. Output File Handling
Test existing output file:
- Verify: Warning about existing file
- Interactive: Prompt for overwrite
- Non-interactive: Proceeds

### 8. Round Number Validation
Invalid round numbers:
- apr run abc = Error: must be positive integer
- apr run -1 = Error: must be positive integer
- apr run 0 = Works (edge case)
- apr run 999 = Works
Shorthand: apr 5 equivalent to apr run 5

### 9. Verbose Mode
apr run 1 --dry-run --verbose
Verify verbose output includes:
- Config loading details
- Option parsing details
- File validation details

### 10. Quiet Mode
apr run 1 --dry-run --quiet
Verify minimal output (errors only)

### 11. run_oracle_with_retry() (NEW)
Test the Oracle execution wrapper:
- Retry logic on transient failures
- Heartbeat option passed
- Notify option passed
- Write-output option passed
- Background execution handling
- PID tracking for robot mode

### 12. Session Slug Generation (NEW)
Verify slug format:
- apr-{workflow}-round-{N}
- apr-{workflow}-round-{N}-with-impl (when --include-impl)
- Special characters handled in workflow name

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] dry-run shows complete Oracle command
- [ ] render outputs prompt correctly
- [ ] Preflight validation working
- [ ] --no-preflight skips checks
- [ ] --include-impl flag working
- [ ] Workflow selection working
- [ ] Round number validation correct
- [ ] Verbose/quiet modes working
- [ ] run_oracle_with_retry tested
- [ ] Session slug format verified

 {"id":"automated_plan_reviser_pro-2uc","title":"Unit Tests: Preflight and Validation Functions","description":"# Task: Unit Tests for Preflight and Validation Functions

## Objective
Test pre-run validation with REAL file system checks.

## Functions to Test

### 1. preflight_check() - Pre-flight Validation
```bash
# Test cases with real files:
# Setup:
mkdir -p /tmp/apr_test
echo "# README" > /tmp/apr_test/README.md
echo "# SPEC" > /tmp/apr_test/SPEC.md
echo "# IMPL" > /tmp/apr_test/IMPL.md

# Happy path:
- All required files exist → return 0
- Oracle available → success message

# File validation:
- README missing → return 1, error message
- README not readable (chmod 000) → return 1
- Spec missing → return 1
- Spec not readable → return 1

# Implementation file (optional):
- impl_path provided but missing → return 2 (warning)
- impl_path provided but not readable → return 2 (warning)
- impl_path provided and valid → return 0

# Oracle checks:
- Oracle not available → return 1
- Oracle version check fails → return 2 (warning)

# Output verification:
- File sizes displayed correctly
- Appropriate success/error/warning messages
```

### 2. run_round() Validation Path
```bash
# Test the validation within run_round:
- Missing workflow config → friendly welcome or error
- Missing required documents → error
- include_impl but no impl configured → warning
- include_impl but impl file missing → warning, continue without
- Output file exists → prompt for overwrite
```

### 3. Robot Mode Validation (robot_validate)
```bash
# Test cases:
- Round number missing → error JSON
- Not initialized → error JSON
- Workflow not found → error JSON
- README missing → errors array populated
- Spec missing → errors array populated
- Oracle not available → errors array populated
- Previous round missing → warnings array (not error)
- All valid → valid:true response
```

## Test Fixtures Required
```
tests/fixtures/documents/
├── valid_readme.md      # Well-formed README
├── valid_spec.md        # Well-formed spec
├── valid_impl.md        # Well-formed implementation
├── empty.md             # Empty file
└── binary_file          # Non-text file
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] preflight_check return codes correct
- [ ] All file checks use real filesystem
- [ ] Error messages helpful and specific
- [ ] Warning vs error distinction correct
- [ ] robot_validate JSON output valid
- [ ] Verbose logging for debugging","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-12T20:09:15.467431863-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-12T20:09:15.467431863-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"automated_plan_reviser_pro-2uc","depends_on_id":"automated_plan_reviser_pro-hqt","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-12T20:12:37.357618897-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

## Objective
Test the complete setup wizard flow with real file creation.

## Test Scenarios

### 1. First-Time Setup (Interactive)
```bash
# Use expect or similar for interactive testing
# Flow:
1. apr setup (with no existing .apr)
2. Enter workflow name
3. Select/enter README path
4. Select/enter spec path  
5. Optionally select implementation path
6. Select GPT model
7. Verify:
   - .apr/config.yaml created
   - .apr/workflows/<name>.yaml created
   - .apr/rounds/<name>/ directory created
   - Config contains correct paths
   - Default workflow set in config.yaml

# Automated version using predefined inputs:
echo -e "test-workflow\n\nREADME.md\nSPEC.md\n\n5.2 Thinking" | apr setup
```

### 2. Additional Workflow Setup
```bash
# With existing .apr directory:
1. apr setup
2. Create second workflow
3. Verify:
   - New workflow config created
   - Default workflow unchanged
   - Both workflows listed by apr list
```

### 3. Setup with Implementation Doc
```bash
# Flow including implementation:
1. apr setup
2. Provide all three document paths
3. Verify implementation path in config
```

### 4. Setup Validation
```bash
# Test validation during setup:
- Workflow name with spaces → handled
- Workflow name with special chars → handled or rejected
- Non-existent file paths → warning/error
- Empty workflow name → error
```

### 5. Gum vs ANSI Modes
```bash
# Test both UI modes:
APR_NO_GUM=1 apr setup  # Test ANSI fallback
apr setup               # Test gum mode (if available)
```

## Test Implementation
```bash
@test "setup creates correct directory structure" {
    cd "$TEST_DIR"
    mkdir project && cd project
    echo "# README" > README.md
    echo "# SPEC" > SPECIFICATION.md
    
    # Simulate interactive input
    run bash -c 'echo -e "myworkflow\n\nREADME.md\nSPECIFICATION.md\n\n5.2 Thinking" | apr setup'
    
    log_test_output "$output"
    
    assert_success
    assert [ -d ".apr" ]
    assert [ -f ".apr/config.yaml" ]
    assert [ -f ".apr/workflows/myworkflow.yaml" ]
    assert [ -d ".apr/rounds/myworkflow" ]
    
    # Verify config content
    assert grep -q "default_workflow: myworkflow" .apr/config.yaml
    assert grep -q "readme: README.md" .apr/workflows/myworkflow.yaml
}
```

```

├── sample_readme.md    # Sample README for testing
├── sample_spec.md      # Sample specification
└── sample_impl.md      # Sample implementation
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fresh setup creates all required files/dirs
- [ ] Additional workflow setup works
- [ ] Implementation doc optional and works
- [ ] Both gum and ANSI modes tested
- [ ] Validation catches invalid inputs
- [ ] Generated config is valid YAML

 {"id":"automated_plan_reviser_pro-8ij","title":"Unit Tests: XDG Compliance and Path Functions","description":"# Task: Unit Tests for XDG Compliance and Path Functions

## Objective
Test that APR correctly uses XDG Base Directory paths and path utility functions.

## Functions to Test

### 1. XDG Path Configuration
```bash
# APR should use:
# - Data: ${XDG_DATA_HOME:-$HOME/.local/share}/apr/
# - Cache: ${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/apr/
# - Config: Per-project .apr/ directory

# Test cases:
- XDG_DATA_HOME set → data goes there
- XDG_DATA_HOME unset → uses ~/.local/share/apr/
- XDG_CACHE_HOME set → cache goes there
- XDG_CACHE_HOME unset → uses ~/.cache/apr/

# Directory creation:
- Data directory created if missing
- Cache directory created if missing
- Proper permissions (700 or 755)
```

### 2. APR_HOME Variable
```bash
# Test cases:
- APR_HOME set → overrides default
- APR_HOME unset → uses XDG_DATA_HOME
- APR_HOME used for:
  - Last update check timestamp
  - Downloaded updates cache
  - Any persistent data
```

### 3. stat_portable() - Cross-Platform stat
```bash
# Test cases (CRITICAL for macOS/Linux):
- GNU stat available → uses %Y format
- BSD stat available → uses -f %m format
- Returns modification time as Unix timestamp
- Works on files
- Works on directories
- Handles missing files gracefully
```

### 4. get_terminal_width() - Terminal Size Detection
```bash
# Test cases:
- tput available → uses tput cols
- tput unavailable → falls back to 80
- Respects minimum width
- Respects maximum width
- Non-TTY returns default
```

### 5. get_script_dir() - Script Location
```bash
# Test cases:
- Returns directory containing apr script
- Works when script is symlinked
- Works with absolute path invocation
- Works with relative path invocation
```

### 6. realpath_portable() if it exists
```bash
# Test cases:
- Resolves symlinks
- Works without realpath binary (fallback)
- Handles relative paths
- Handles paths with spaces
```

## Test Implementation
```bash
@test "XDG data home is respected" {
    export XDG_DATA_HOME="/tmp/test_xdg_data"
    mkdir -p "$XDG_DATA_HOME"
    
    source apr
    
    # Trigger something that uses APR_HOME
    # ...
    
    assert [ -d "$XDG_DATA_HOME/apr" ]
}

@test "stat_portable works on Linux and macOS" {
    local test_file=$(mktemp)
    
    source apr
    local mtime=$(stat_portable "$test_file")
    
    # Should be a valid Unix timestamp
    assert [ "$mtime" -gt 1000000000 ]
    
    rm "$test_file"
}
```

## Cross-Platform Testing
- Test on Ubuntu (GNU coreutils)
- Test on macOS (BSD stat)
- Verify no bashisms that break Bash 4.0

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] XDG_DATA_HOME respected
- [ ] XDG_CACHE_HOME respected
- [ ] APR_HOME override works
- [ ] stat_portable works on both platforms
- [ ] get_terminal_width handles all cases
- [ ] Path functions work with special characters

 {"id":"automated_plan_reviser_pro-ayr","title":"Unit Tests: Utility Functions (version_gt, can_prompt, iso_timestamp)","description":"# Task: Unit Tests for Utility Functions

## Objective
Create comprehensive unit tests for all utility functions in APR without using mocks.

## Functions to Test

### 1. version_gt() - Semantic Version Comparison
Test cases:
- version_gt 1.2.0 1.1.0 = true (minor bump)
- version_gt 2.0.0 1.9.9 = true (major bump)
- version_gt 1.0.1 1.0.0 = true (patch bump)
- version_gt 1.0.0 1.0.0 = false (equal)
- version_gt 1.0.0 1.0.1 = false (older)
- version_gt 1.10.0 1.9.0 = true (double digit)
- version_gt 0.1.0 0.0.9 = true (zero major)
Edge cases:
- Empty strings
- Invalid version formats
- Sort -V fallback vs string comparison

### 2. can_prompt() - Interactive Terminal Detection
Test cases:
- TTY on stdin and stderr = true
- No TTY on stdin = false
- No TTY on stderr = false
- QUIET_MODE=true = false
Real testing approach:
- Use script(1) or expect to simulate TTY
- Test with actual pipes/redirects

### 3. iso_timestamp() - ISO8601 Timestamp
Test cases:
- Format matches YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ
- Timezone is UTC (Z suffix)
- Incrementing calls show time progression

### 4. verbose() - Debug Logging
Test cases:
- VERBOSE=false = no output
- VERBOSE=true = output to stderr
- Output format includes [apr:verbose] prefix

### 5. check_gum() - Gum Availability (NEW)
Test cases:
- gum in PATH = GUM_AVAILABLE=true
- gum not in PATH = GUM_AVAILABLE=false
- APR_NO_GUM=1 = GUM_AVAILABLE=false even if gum exists
- CI=true = gum suppressed
- GITHUB_ACTIONS=true = gum suppressed

### 6. check_oracle() - Oracle Availability (NEW)
Test cases:
- oracle command in PATH = returns 0, method=global
- oracle not in PATH but npx available = returns 0, method=npx
- Neither available = returns 1
- Sets ORACLE_CMD correctly

### 7. try_install_gum() - Gum Installation (NEW)
Test cases:
- Package manager detection (brew, apt, etc.)
- Interactive prompt for installation
- Non-interactive mode skips
- Installation failure handling

 {"id":"automated_plan_reviser_pro-de5","title":"Unit Tests: Robot Mode JSON Functions (robot_json, robot_status, robot_workflows, etc.)","description":"# Task: Unit Tests for Robot Mode Functions

## Objective
Test all robot mode functions that produce JSON output.

## Functions to Test

### 1. robot_json() - JSON Response Builder
```bash
# Test cases:
- ok=true, code="ok" → valid JSON envelope
- ok=false, code="error" → valid JSON envelope
- hint parameter included when provided
- hint parameter omitted when empty
- meta.v contains VERSION
- meta.ts is valid ISO8601
- ROBOT_COMPACT=true → minified output
- ROBOT_COMPACT=false → pretty-printed

# JSON validation:
- All outputs parseable by jq
- No trailing characters
- Correct escaping of special characters in data
```

## New Display Elements

### 1. Sparklines (Unicode)
Visual trend indicators using block characters:
```bash
# Block characters for sparklines
SPARK_CHARS="▁▂▃▄▅▆▇█"

# Generate sparkline from array of values
generate_sparkline() {
    local -a values=("$@")
    local min max range

    verbose "Generating sparkline for ${#values[@]} values"

    # Find min/max
    min=${values[0]}
    max=${values[0]}
    for v in "${values[@]}"; do
        (( v < min )) && min=$v
        (( v > max )) && max=$v
    done

    range=$((max - min))
    [[ $range -eq 0 ]] && range=1  # Avoid division by zero

    verbose "Sparkline range: min=$min max=$max range=$range"

    local sparkline=""
    for v in "${values[@]}"; do
        local idx=$(( (v - min) * 7 / range ))
        sparkline+="${SPARK_CHARS:$idx:1}"
    done

    echo "$sparkline"
}
```

## Robot Mode Support

**CRITICAL**: The stats command must support robot mode for programmatic access.

```bash
# Add robot_stats function
robot_stats() {
    local workflow="${WORKFLOW:-default}"

    verbose "Robot mode: stats for workflow $workflow"

    # Check if metrics exist
    if ! metrics_exists "$workflow"; then
        verbose "No metrics found for workflow: $workflow"
        json_response "error" "no_metrics" "{}" \
            "No analytics data. Run 'apr backfill' or complete some rounds."
        return
    fi

    # Read metrics
    local metrics
    metrics=$(metrics_read "$workflow")

    verbose "Read metrics, building response"

    # Build response
    local round_count avg_size convergence
    round_count=$(echo "$metrics" | jq '.rounds | length')
    avg_size=$(echo "$metrics" | jq '[.rounds[].output.char_count // 0] | add / length | floor')
    convergence=$(echo "$metrics" | jq '.convergence // {}')

    local data
    data=$(jq -nc \
        --arg workflow "$workflow" \
        --argjson round_count "$round_count" \
        --argjson avg_size "$avg_size" \
        --argjson convergence "$convergence" \
        --argjson rounds "$(echo "$metrics" | jq '.rounds')" \
        '{
            workflow: $workflow,
            round_count: $round_count,
            average_output_size: $avg_size,
            convergence: $convergence,
            rounds: $rounds
        }')

    json_response "ok" "ok" "$data"
}
```

## Graceful Degradation

**IMPORTANT**: The stats command must handle partial or missing data gracefully.

### Degradation Levels

```bash
show_stats() {
    local workflow="${WORKFLOW:-default}"
    local detailed="${STATS_DETAILED:-false}"

    verbose "Showing stats for workflow: $workflow (detailed=$detailed)"

    # Level 1: No rounds exist
    if ! has_rounds "$workflow"; then
        verbose "No rounds found - showing basic info"
        show_stats_no_rounds "$workflow"
        return
    fi

    # Level 2: Rounds exist but no metrics
    if ! metrics_exists "$workflow"; then
        verbose "Rounds exist but no metrics - showing basic stats"
        show_stats_basic "$workflow"
        print_info "Run 'apr backfill' to generate rich analytics"
        return
    fi

    # Level 3: Metrics exist but incomplete (backfilled)
    local metrics
    metrics=$(metrics_read "$workflow")
    local has_full_data
    has_full_data=$(echo "$metrics" | jq 'any(.rounds[]; .backfilled == true) | not')

    if [[ "$has_full_data" == "false" ]]; then
        verbose "Metrics are backfilled (partial) - showing with caveats"
        show_stats_backfilled "$workflow" "$metrics"
        return
    fi

    # Level 4: Full metrics available
    verbose "Full metrics available - showing rich stats"
    show_stats_full "$workflow" "$metrics"
}

# Level 1: No rounds
show_stats_no_rounds() {
    local workflow="$1"
    print_banner
    print_header "REVISION STATISTICS: $workflow"
    echo "" >&2
    print_warning "No rounds completed yet"
    print_info "Run 'apr run 1' to start your first revision round"
}

# Level 2: Basic (no metrics)
show_stats_basic() {
    local workflow="$1"
    print_banner
    print_header "REVISION STATISTICS: $workflow (basic)"
    echo "" >&2
    # ... show file-based stats only (size, date from files)
}

# Level 3: Backfilled (partial metrics)
show_stats_backfilled() {
    local workflow="$1"
    local metrics="$2"
    print_banner
    print_header "REVISION STATISTICS: $workflow"
    echo "" >&2
    print_dim "Note: Some data backfilled from existing rounds (limited accuracy)"
    # ... show metrics but with caveats for document metrics
}

# Level 4: Full metrics
show_stats_full() {
    local workflow="$1"
    local metrics="$2"
    # ... full rich display
}
```

### 1. Convergence Gauge
Large visual indicator of convergence status:
```bash
render_convergence_gauge() {
    local score="$1"
    local percentage=$((score * 100 / 1))
    
    # ASCII art gauge
    local filled=$((percentage / 5))  # 20 segments
    local empty=$((20 - filled))
    
    printf "  ["
    printf '%*s' "$filled" | tr ' ' '█'
    printf '%*s' "$empty" | tr ' ' '░'
    printf "] %d%%\n" "$percentage"
}
```

### 2. Bar Chart for Output Size
```bash
render_bar_chart() {
    local -a values=("$@")
    local max_val=${values[0]}
    local max_height=5
    
    # Find max
    for v in "${values[@]}"; do
        (( v > max_val )) && max_val=$v
    done
    
    # Render rows from top to bottom
    for ((row=max_height; row>=1; row--)); do
        local threshold=$((max_val * row / max_height))
        for v in "${values[@]}"; do
            if (( v >= threshold )); then
                printf " ██"
            else
                printf "   "
            fi
        done
        printf "\n"
    done
    
    # X-axis labels
    for ((i=1; i<=${#values[@]}; i++)); do
        printf " R%d" "$i"
    done
    printf "\n"
}
```

```bash
}
```

```bash
    local running=true
    
    # Hide cursor
    printf '\033[?25l'
    trap 'printf "\033[?25h"; clear' EXIT
```

```bash
# Add to stats command
export_metrics() {
    local workflow="$1"
    local format="${2:-json}"
    local output_file="${3:-}"
    
    local metrics
    metrics=$(metrics_read "$workflow")
    
    case "$format" in
        json)
            export_json "$metrics" "$output_file"
            ;;
        csv)
            export_csv "$metrics" "$output_file"
            ;;
        md|markdown)
            export_markdown "$metrics" "$output_file"
            ;;
        *)
            print_error "Unknown export format: $format"
            print_info "Supported formats: json, csv, md"
            return 1
            ;;
    esac
}
```

```bash
export_csv() {
    local metrics="$1"

            (.changes_from_previous.similarity_score // ""),

        echo "$csv_output" > "$output_file"

    else
        echo "$csv_output"
    fi
}
```

## Context

Users who have already run APR rounds before the analytics feature won't have metrics data. This command retroactively collects metrics from existing round files.

## Dependencies
- **Requires**: automated_plan_reviser_pro-fzi.3 (document metrics collection)
- **Requires**: automated_plan_reviser_pro-fzi.4 (change analysis)

## Use Case

```bash
# User has existing rounds but no metrics
$ ls .apr/rounds/default/
round_1.md  round_2.md  round_3.md  round_4.md  round_5.md

$ apr stats
⚠ No analytics data. Run 'apr backfill' to generate metrics from existing rounds.

$ apr backfill
ℹ Backfilling metrics for workflow 'default'...
  Analyzing round 1...
  Analyzing round 2...
  Analyzing round 3...
  Analyzing round 4...
  Analyzing round 5...
✓ Backfilled 5 rounds
✓ Calculated convergence metrics

$ apr stats
# Now shows full analytics
```

## Command Design

```bash
apr backfill [workflow] [options]

Options:
  -w, --workflow NAME    Workflow to backfill (default: current default)
  --all                  Backfill all workflows
  --force                Overwrite existing metrics
  --dry-run              Show what would be done
```

## Implementation

```bash
cmd_backfill() {
    local workflow="${1:-$WORKFLOW}"
    local all="${BACKFILL_ALL:-false}"
    local force="${BACKFILL_FORCE:-false}"
    local dry_run="${DRY_RUN:-false}"
    
    if [[ "$all" == "true" ]]; then
        backfill_all_workflows "$force" "$dry_run"
    else
        backfill_workflow "$workflow" "$force" "$dry_run"
    fi
}

backfill_workflow() {
    local workflow="$1"
    local force="$2"
    local dry_run="$3"
    
    local rounds_dir="$CONFIG_DIR/rounds/$workflow"
    local workflow_file="$CONFIG_DIR/workflows/${workflow}.yaml"
    
    # Validate
    if [[ ! -d "$rounds_dir" ]]; then
        print_error "No rounds found for workflow '$workflow'"
        return 1
    fi
    
    if [[ ! -f "$workflow_file" ]]; then
        print_error "Workflow config not found: $workflow_file"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Check if metrics already exist
    if metrics_exists "$workflow" && [[ "$force" != "true" ]]; then
        print_warning "Metrics already exist for '$workflow'"
        print_info "Use --force to overwrite"
        return 1
    fi
    
    if [[ "$dry_run" == "true" ]]; then
        print_info "[DRY RUN] Would backfill workflow '$workflow'"
    fi
    
    print_info "Backfilling metrics for workflow '$workflow'..."
    
    # Get document paths from workflow config
    local readme_path spec_path impl_path
    readme_path=$(get_config_value "readme" "$workflow_file" | tr -d '"')
    spec_path=$(get_config_value "spec" "$workflow_file" | tr -d '"')
    impl_path=$(get_config_value "implementation" "$workflow_file" | tr -d '"')
    
    # Initialize metrics file
    if [[ "$dry_run" != "true" ]]; then
        metrics_init "$workflow"
    fi
    
    # Get sorted round files
    local sorted_files=()
    while IFS= read -r round_file; do
        sorted_files+=("$round_file")
    done < <(find "$rounds_dir" -maxdepth 1 -name "round_*.md" -type f | sort -t_ -k2 -n)
    
    local count=0
    local prev_output=""
    
    for round_file in "${sorted_files[@]}"; do
        local round_num
        round_num=$(basename "$round_file" .md | sed 's/round_//')
        
        print_dim "  Analyzing round $round_num..."
        
        if [[ "$dry_run" == "true" ]]; then
            ((count++))
            continue
        fi
        
        # Collect output metrics
        local output_metrics
        output_metrics=$(collect_document_metrics "$round_file" "output")
        
        # Get timestamp from file modification time
        local file_ts
        file_ts=$(stat -c '%Y' "$round_file" 2>/dev/null || stat -f '%m' "$round_file" 2>/dev/null || echo "0")
        local timestamp
        timestamp=$(date -d "@$file_ts" -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ" 2>/dev/null || \
                   date -r "$file_ts" -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ" 2>/dev/null || \
                   echo "1970-01-01T00:00:00Z")
        
        # Calculate changes from previous (if exists)
        local change_metrics="null"
        if [[ -n "$prev_output" && -f "$prev_output" ]]; then
            change_metrics=$(calculate_change_metrics "$prev_output" "$round_file")
        fi
        
        # Note: We can't accurately backfill document metrics for README/spec/impl
        # because we don't know what state they were in when each round was run.
        # We can only capture output metrics and inter-output changes.
        
        # Build round record
        local round_record
        round_record=$(jq -nc \
            --argjson round "$round_num" \
            --arg timestamp "$timestamp" \
            --argjson output "$output_metrics" \
            --argjson changes "$change_metrics" \
            '{
                round: $round,
                timestamp: $timestamp,
                documents: null,
                output: $output,
                changes_from_previous: $changes,
                backfilled: true
            }')
        
        metrics_write_round "$workflow" "$round_num" "$round_record"
        
        prev_output="$round_file"
        ((count++))
    done
    
    if [[ "$dry_run" == "true" ]]; then
        print_info "[DRY RUN] Would backfill $count rounds"
        return 0
    fi
    
    # Calculate convergence
    verbose "Calculating convergence metrics..."
    update_convergence_metrics "$workflow"
    
    print_success "Backfilled $count rounds"
    print_success "Calculated convergence metrics"
}

backfill_all_workflows() {
    local force="$1"
    local dry_run="$2"
    
    local workflow_dir="$CONFIG_DIR/workflows"
    if [[ ! -d "$workflow_dir" ]]; then
        print_error "No workflows configured"
        return 1
    fi
    
    local count=0
    for config in "$workflow_dir"/*.yaml; do
        [[ -f "$config" ]] || continue
        local name
        name=$(basename "$config" .yaml)
        
        backfill_workflow "$name" "$force" "$dry_run"
        ((count++))
    done
    
    print_success "Processed $count workflow(s)"
}
```

## Limitations

### What CAN be backfilled:
- GPT output file metrics (size, structure)
- Inter-round output changes (diff metrics)
- Timestamps (from file modification time)

### What CANNOT be backfilled:
- Input document metrics at time of round (README, spec changed since)
- Accurate timestamps if files were copied/moved
- Any data not derivable from round output files

### Marking Backfilled Rounds
Add `"backfilled": true` flag to indicate data limitations:
```json
{
  "round": 3,
  "backfilled": true,
  "documents": null,  // Unknown at backfill time
  "output": { ... },  // Can calculate
  "changes_from_previous": { ... }  // Can calculate
}
```

## Integration

Add to main() command routing:
```bash
backfill)
    cmd_backfill "${positional_args[@]}"
    ;;
```

Add help text:
```bash
echo "    ${GREEN}backfill${NC}           Generate metrics from existing rounds" >&2
```

2. [ ] `--all` backfills all workflows
3. [ ] `--force` overwrites existing metrics
4. [ ] `--dry-run` shows what would happen
5. [ ] Output metrics collected correctly
6. [ ] Change metrics calculated correctly
7. [ ] Rounds processed in numeric order
8. [ ] Timestamps derived from file mtime
9. [ ] `backfilled: true` flag set
10. [ ] Convergence calculated after backfill

```bash
        metrics_write_round "$workflow" "$round_num" "$round_record"
        
        prev_output="$round_file"
        ((count++))
    done
    
    if [[ "$dry_run" == "true" ]]; then
        print_info "[DRY RUN] Would backfill $count rounds"
        return 0
    fi
    
    # Calculate convergence
    verbose "Calculating convergence metrics..."
    update_convergence_metrics "$workflow"
    
    print_success "Backfilled $count rounds"
    print_success "Calculated convergence metrics"
}

backfill_all_workflows() {
    local force="$1"
    local dry_run="$2"
    
    local workflow_dir="$CONFIG_DIR/workflows"
    if [[ ! -d "$workflow_dir" ]]; then
        print_error "No workflows configured"
        return 1
    fi
    
    local count=0
    for config in "$workflow_dir"/*.yaml; do
        [[ -f "$config" ]] || continue
        local name
        name=$(basename "$config" .yaml)
        
        backfill_workflow "$name" "$force" "$dry_run"
        ((count++))
    done
    
    print_success "Processed $count workflow(s)"
}
```

## Limitations

### What CAN be backfilled:
- GPT output file metrics (size, structure)
- Inter-round output changes (diff metrics)
- Timestamps (from file modification time)

### What CANNOT be backfilled:
- Input document metrics at time of round (README, spec changed since)
- Accurate timestamps if files were copied/moved
- Any data not derivable from round output files

### Marking Backfilled Rounds
Add `"backfilled": true` flag to indicate data limitations:
```json
{
  "round": 3,
  "backfilled": true,
  "documents": null,  // Unknown at backfill time
  "output": { ... },  // Can calculate
  "changes_from_previous": { ... }  // Can calculate
}
```

## Integration

Add to main() command routing:
```bash
backfill)
    cmd_backfill "${positional_args[@]}"
    ;;
```

Add help text:
```bash
echo "    ${GREEN}backfill${NC}           Generate metrics from existing rounds" >&2
```
